title: Miller's power gambit to return Westpac to glory
full_text:
Financial services The boss of Australia's oldest bank understands the importance of politics to its success, writes Angira Bharadwaj.
As lunch dates go, it could have been an awkward one. But if there was any tension between Westpac boss Anthony Miller and dining companions Anthony Albanese and Jim Chalmers the day after the May budget you would not have noticed.
The treasurer's fifth federal budget contained a new minimum capital gains tax across all investments and a winding back of negative gearing on property investments - measures that both threaten to choke the banks' key revenue streams.
The potential negative ramifications were laid bare by the 10 per cent plunge in Commonwealth Bank's share price the following morning when the bank delivered a trading update. Westpac's share price was spared, but bank analysts slashed their credit growth forecasts for the sector.
But it was all smiles at Chalmers' post-budget lunch at the National Press Club, with Miller scoring a prime spot between the treasurer and prime minister, on a table also including Finance Minister Katy Gallagher and Deputy Prime Minister Richard Marles.
It helped that Westpac was sponsoring the event, giving Miller valuable access to the nation's top policymakers in one of the most pivotal weeks of the political calendar.
Since taking on the chief executive role in December 2024, Miller has made no secret of his ambition to restore Westpac, Australia's second-largest mortgage lender, to greatness. For many, he is emerging as the natural successor to Matt Comyn as the unofficial head of Australian banking when the CBA chief finally decides to step down from the helm of the nation's biggest lender.
Miller has been building on the reputational repair work done by his predecessor Peter King in the dark days following the Hayne royal commission and also the money-laundering scandal for which the bank copped a $1.3 billion fine. The latter scandal claimed the scalps of then-Westpac chief executive Brian Hartzer and chairman Lindsay Maxsted.
A key part of Miller's strategy has been to build strong relationships with top policymakers, even if they don't always see eye to eye.
Those close to him say Miller is acutely aware of his position as chief custodian of the country's oldest company and first bank, and passionate about its role in nation-building.
"He understands that when Australia succeeds, Westpac's customers, shareholders, and stakeholders also succeed," says Bran Black, who leads the Business Council of Australia.
"That's why I think he's been so willing to speak up on major national challenges like productivity, housing supply and our economic growth trajectory."
Miller is a strong backer of Westpac's long-running sponsorship of the National Press Club, and the bank also hosted two tables at the Canberra's Midwinter Ball this month. Both are forums that offer access to Canberra's top powerbrokers.
At Chalmers' productivity roundtable in August, Miller was an influential voice, raising ideas such as growing the population of regional Australia by a million people by 2032 and using artificial intelligence to fast-track lagging housing approvals.
Anna Bligh, a former chief executive of the Australian Banking Association and an ex-premier of Queensland, says government and political engagement was an increasingly important part of the job for chief executives.
"Whether you're a bank or a supermarket or a telco ... those relationships are critical to being a good CEO," she says. "When you do want to make big changes, you've got a base from which to explain those big changes [to government]."
Those familiar with Westpac's strategy under Miller say the former Goldman Sachs investment banker was initially taken aback by the level of political engagement required by the major bank CEOs.
But he has stepped up in a way that few Westpac chief executives have done since ex-Treasury official David Morgan ran the bank.
Miller's developed a strong relationship with Department of Prime Minister and Cabinet secretary Steven Kennedy, the nation's most powerful bureaucrat, in way that reminds David Gazard, Westpac's head of government relations in the early 2000s, of his former boss.
"Morgan would have had the connections with Ken Henry who was secretary of Treasury at the time ... He understood how government worked," Gazard says.
Cultivating those political connections will be crucial as the industry faces challenges across a number of fronts.
"It's all well and good to be in a bromance with the treasurer, but you have you be able to impact policy," says Gazard, who has run his own lobbying firm for many years. "I see banking at the moment being highly fraught. What is AI going to do for banking, for example? What are the tax changes going to do? You have to be explaining what the dynamics are and influencing policy."
Westpac investor and Ten Cap portfolio manager Jun Bei Liu says Miller's efforts to bolster engagement with government made sense given the outsized impact its policies can have on the banks' revenues. "They are heavily regulated and a barometer for the economy ... banks all want to be seen as the friendly with the government."
However, others say there was a limit to how much influence bank chief executives could exert, as evidenced by the new tax settings in the budget that analysts predict could halve credit growth and end a long-running property super cycle.
"I don't think banks can drive public policy in this era like the changes to CGT and negative gearing," says veteran banking analyst Brian Johnson from MST Marquee. "I'm pretty sure the banks wouldn't have wanted to see it come through, both the timing and overall mechanics of it."
But Johnson still sees merit in Miller's approach, adding: "The more constructive engagement you can have from private sector banks and the government and regulators, the better."
Miller's relationship with the government has been strengthened by a number of initiatives under his watch. They include being the only major lender to extend a moratorium on closing regional bank branches to 2030, a move the federal government has called on others to follow.
Westpac has opened a handful of new regional branches - catnip to rural and regional MPs who are concerned about access to cash and services in the bush - as well as introducing drive-in-drive-out bankers to work out of community halls in towns that no longer have a physical branch.
These are low-cost moves that have helped to raise Westpac's standing in the government's eyes.
Fraud and scam prevention is another hot-button issue in which Westpac has been working closely with the government, led by retail bank chief Carolyn McCann, while institutional bank head Nell Hutton regularly briefed officials on the impact of rising petrol prices on Westpac's customers during the Middle East conflict.
It has been slower on its engagement on artificial intelligence policy, however, in which CBA dominates.
At the same time, Miller has been critical of some of the government's policies, privately telling senior Labor figures that public spending is out of control, according to one source.
He has also questioned the budget decision to wind back generous tax settings on shares in addition to housing.
"I think continuing to incentivise and reward people for investing in shares is a good thing," he said on The Pay Off podcast. "Until you can buy a house, what are those other investment vehicles that you might pursue?"
Another industry source familiar with Westpac's strategy says Miller has more freedom to weigh in on policy debates and shape the conversation in Canberra because the bank's core business has been in decent shape.
"If you look at Peter King [Miller's predecessor] he was absolutely having to focus on a whole range of issues including consolidation and regulatory issues ... Miller has got a bit of freedom to focus on those policy issues," they say. "Five or six years ago, the reputation of Westpac in Canberra was mixed, whereas now you universally get some kind of positive."
Another industry source agrees, saying King had resurrected the business in the wake of the Hayne royal commission and "gifted Anthony a company that had put a lot of the bad stuff behind it".
They add that Miller's gift of the gab has helped build on King's work.
"Anthony has a very down-to-earth style," they say of Miller's public appearances. "He relates to the punters pretty well."
Media is another medium Miller is using to build his brand, and was doing the rounds with multiple spots on radio following the budget, discussing the negative gearing and CGT changes, and the potential impact on housing affordability, the need for more supply, as well as the imperative for improving productivity.
Those who have worked in banking for a long time know the benefits of a chief executive who can perform in the media and get on with politicians.
One long-time banker points to former ANZ chief executive John McFarlane, who was dubbed "Johnny Hollywood" in the papers for his charisma. Others who performed well in those arenas include former CBA chief David Murray and one of Miller's predecessors at Westpac, Gail Kelly.
With his star on the rise, it is little surprise that many in the industry see Miller emerging as the pre-eminent spokesman for the banking industry as CBA's Comyn enters the home straight of his tenure.
The Westpac boss scored a notable coup over Comyn last year when the bank snared the sponsoring rights to Australian cricket from CBA, just in time for the 2025-26 Ashes series, which saw the big red W emblazoned across TV screens and mobile devices as the men's team romped to victory.
It also helps that Miller's other two rivals, National Australia Bank's Andrew Irvine and ANZ's Nuno Matos, are foreigners and new to the Australian market.
Both, arguably, have more pressing priorities internally, giving Miller clear air to establish his industry leadership credentials.
Those close to Miller say he is a different operator from Comyn, preferring a behind-the-scenes approach to relationship building with government as opposed to the CBA boss's very public bromance with Chalmers - but he's no less effective for that.
"There is a lot more happening behind the scenes than people know about. It's like the duck legs below water," one source close to Miller says.
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Angira Bharadwaj
heading: Banking and Economy
